Welcome to week one of the Momentum Builder Workshop Lean Edition. Week one is going to be an overview. We're going to talk about strategy. And we're also going to talk about the thematic arc that connects all of the things that we'll be working on in this workshop together. That's really the secret sauce behind many of our campaigns, certainly our most successful campaigns. And we're going to go over that in today's lesson. Many of you have been with us for a long time are wondering what in the world is happening. This isn't written. This is a presentation. It's not something Andre and I usually do. But for this first module in particular, we really need to be able to show you the process step by step. This has been a sort of a crazy journey. This started with thinking it would be a whiteboard video, which was a miserable experience. And then it became sketching on an iPad and capturing the screen, which was an equally miserable experience. And then there were a couple of days of horizontal whiteboard with video, which I recorded this entire presentation with no audio in one version. And now here we are doing, I think, I think all of those roads got us to the place where we are now, which is far better. So, and I hope you agree. So let's jump in. Everything that we're going to talk about for the next five weeks revolves around you being able to generate more happy customers. And both of those words are important, happy and customer. How you define happy, really, that's going to be up to you when you're a particular business and there are metrics we could use. But really at the end of the day, we know it when we see it. If you generate 100 customers and 50% of them want to refund, those aren't happy customers. So we want happy customers, but we also want customers. Yes, you could use everything that you're going to learn in this workshop to validate an idea without somebody needing to pay money at the end. So you could take them through this entire journey and you could get them to sign up for something to do a free course. And if that works for you, then that's perfectly okay. Money doesn't have to change hands for this to be valuable. The reason we think it is important, if you can make it so that money changes hands, is that that's a much stronger signal. We don't want to get caught up in this idea about money and about how much do I charge and is it worth it. We don't, that's not the focus of this workshop. And I hope that's not your focus either. That money is a neutral indicator of value. When we charge money for something, it is we get a better, cleaner, stronger signal from the marketplace. So when we talk about customers and we talk about offers and pricing and other things, we're going to assume that you at the end of this journey, at the end of this process, you are turning the attention that you have generated and earned from prospects into dollars for your business in an exchange of value. The other side of the spectrum, of course, are prospects. And when we think about prospects, we're going to think about Facebook as being the source of where we get them, although that may not be, that's not a requirement. That's what we'll focus on for this workshop. But you can think about your prospects if you have an email list. You could use the content in the lessons in this workshop for a variety of things. I think because we're focusing on a cold audience first, meaning an audience that doesn't know you, that you'll get a larger benefit than you would get if we assume that the audience knows you in advance. So let's talk about the characteristics of prospects as we think about them. Your prospects have desires, things that they want, they have fears, things that they don't want to have happen. And using Clayton Christiansen's terms, they have jobs to be done. And those jobs have three broad components. They're functional, like you need it to do, like a car, for example, you need it to go from point A to point B. That's a functional job. But jobs also have social and emotional components. Using a vehicle again, a car again. What a Prius says about you, or what a Tesla says about you is different than what a full-size pickup says about you, or what a Lamborghini says about you. So when we buy things, we buy them for reasons other than just the function. So this combination of ideas, desires, fears, and then jobs to be done, that's going to factor in how we think about turning prospects into happy customers. Connecting these two points is really what the subject of this entire workshop is going to be about. And the first element on there is a Facebook ad. We're going to write a 500-ish to 800-ish word Facebook ad, that will be in next week, week two. And those ads have several jobs that they need to accomplish. The first is that they need to attract the right prospect. That's obvious. If we weren't attracting the right prospect, there's no way to make happy customers. But the other thing that the Facebook ad copy needs to do is it needs to repel the wrong prospects. So we're not going to hide the fact, for example, if you're going to sell something later, we're not going to try to diminish the fact that something may be for sale. Or if they need to opt in later, we're not going to try to hide that. Because we don't want to repel people, or we do want to repel people who don't give their email address, for example. If someone says in the very beginning, well, I would never give you my email address. Well, that's good to know. We don't want to get their attention. And we'll go in more detail in a little while about the Facebook ad, and we'll certainly go into much more detail about it next week in the workshop. But for now, what you need to understand is that the Facebook ad is the entry point for somebody who probably doesn't know you at all. This is where they first learn about you. It's the beginning of a journey that they're going to have with you. After the Facebook ad, we're going to send them to a landing page. That landing page will be somewhere in the neighborhood of 1200 to 1800 words. We're imagining it will be a single page with an opt-in at the end. You could do it over two pages. We'll talk about why you might do that. That might be something you do in the future. You might turn a longer landing page into a single landing page, or two landing pages, multi-page landing pages. There will be lots of discussion when we get to week three about the landing page and its characteristics. What's important to know for this conversation is that the landing page continues from where the Facebook ad began. We're not trying to get some attention on a Facebook ad, and then using curiosity or other things, get people to go to a landing page. That linear idea, we're going to sacrifice the linear idea for a much more holistic view of this entire process. In the next step, a relationship building emails. So someone is seeing your Facebook ad. It was interesting for the right reasons. It's compelling. It repelled the wrong people. It attracted the right people. We've continued the conversation on the landing page, and we've continued to earn their attention. We've continued to build a world that's interesting for this person. They've raised their hand to say, I would like more. And then over the course of three emails, that's what we'll focus on in this course. You could make them longer in the future if you'd like. Over the course, in this course, we're going to focus on building or creating three relationship building emails. So after somebody opts in, we're going to have three opportunities to establish trust and build rapport. There's nothing to buy in these emails at first. That's something very important. Not only is there nothing to buy, but we're not even suggesting that there's something to buy. We're building on the theme that we created in the Facebook ad and on the landing page. And we're continuing to open and close loops. That's something that we'll do really from the very beginning, is that we're going to open ideas that get closed later that pulls people forward. And then the last component is going to be a promotional email series. Again, three emails for the purposes of this workshop. This is where we're going to introduce an offer. We're going to build desire for the right prospects. And what's being offered is the next logical step in the relationship. That's really what's important to understand about all of these components, is they all flow together so that the Facebook ad opens up the door to this entire process. And that entire arc is cohesive and it's congruent and it makes sense. So let's talk about now some broad themes about how we're going to do this. I'm going to borrow three ideas from Donald Miller, one of my favorite authors, one of my favorite books is A Million Miles in a Thousand Years. Donald Miller has since created Storybrand and he has a seven-part framework that uses story for marketing. He's borrowing from many others too, of course. So I want to give him credit and I'm sure he's given credit to those before him. But there are three parts of that Storybrand framework in Broad Strokes and three parts of the Storyframe framework in Broad Strokes that we're going to utilize for this workshop. The first one is empathy. Empathy is this idea that you've walked a mile in the shoes of your prospects. You know their desires, you know their fears, you know the jobs that they want to do, not just functionally, but you know the deeper needs behind those, these social needs, the emotional needs. You really understand your prospect. It's possible that you were your prospect at one point in time. So you not only do you know their fears intellectually, but you felt them emotionally. We're going to recognize their frustration overtly. We're going to be clear about it. We're going to name it for them. And most importantly, where empathy fits in is that empathy is going to be our opportunity to make it clear that we care about them. This is not just a financial transaction. We're not trying to get them from point A to point B as quickly as possible so that we can make the sale and move on. Empathy is what allows us to say, I see you. And later we're going to show a solution for them relative to your area of expertise. So your prospects, of course, that we're trying to attract are those that you specifically can help. So that's a critical component. Now after empathy comes authority. So once somebody knows that you feel their pain, the next thing we want them to know is that you have experience dealing with that. That experience could be that you've gone through it yourself and you've developed some skills or other things around how to address their desires, their fears, their jobs to be done. You could have credentials. So you could have been helping people for as a professional as a career, or you could have lots of customers and testimonials and people you've worked with. That combination of empathy and authority shows up really a lot in the first three components of this thematic arc. So the Facebook ad is overwhelmingly empathy. Landing page also overwhelmingly empathy. We might introduce a little authority in those places too, just to make it will depend on your particular situation, but empathy is what we want to lead with. We want to say, I see you. And then as we transition into the relationship building emails, we'll lean a little bit more heavily on authority. We're going to now transition from I understand you, I see you to I've had experience with this and I can help and here are a few things that you may find helpful. But until that point, there's no, we're not selling anything yet to we're taking our time to let our audience know that we understand them and that we can help. And then the third component is a plan. So not only do we understand not only have we walked a mile in their shoes and I only do we have some experience helping them solve the problems that they want to solve or get the does achieve the desires that they want to achieve. We also know what to do. And that's really the transition to the promotional emails and the plan. We dimensionalize the future that they desire. So we say dimensionalize we mean we give it we bring it into HD clarity. So we describe for them the future that our solution can create for them, not just in functional terms, but in emotional terms and social terms and it's really dependent on your particular offer. But we are we're making a much larger case for how we can help them specifically. And we're going to show them the path from where they are their current reality to the desired future reality. That's really where the promotional emails shine is that we're connecting the dots for them. We can demonstrate how it's worked before if you've worked with customers in the past or you have people you've engaged with you've sold a service to or a product to. If we can point to their successes, we can show them how it's worked. And we're also going to be transparent about what is expected of them. We're not just trying to get the sale here. This is not again this is not a rush to get the customer. What we want is the happy customer. So if they need to do something. So if this is for example, let's assume it's a weight loss product. And if in order for them to be successful, they have to commit to the program. Seven days a week, 24 hours a day, then we're going to be honest with them about it. We're going to explain it's not magic that they have to do things to there's no magic pill that they're taking. And but whatever it is for your particular offer, this is where we're going to be transparent about it. Because again, we're not just trying to get the sale as quickly as possible. We want that person who makes the purchase to be happy about having made the purchase. That's how we create super fans. All of this is tied together with a thematic arc and that thematic arc establishes congruence. It pulls prospects forward. The mistake that we've seen made so often and I'm sure all of you listening to this have experienced it in some form or another. Is that you see a Facebook ad and it's maybe it gets your attention for some reason. But then you get your second or third email and things start to feel off a little bit. It's like the person that you met in the Facebook ad. It was a little bit different in the landing page and now they're talking about something entirely different in an email and they're trying to sell me something that's not necessarily even related to what I thought I want. And that dissonance, that sense of disconnect is very jarring and we're we pick up on it very quickly as consumers. So what the thematic arc is going to do is it's going to show us one long journey from beginning to end in that journey. One of the beauties of it is we know where we're taking them. We know what a happy customer is. We know the value that we can create in the world and we're working backwards from that to prospects who have a desire for that outcome that we're able to produce. And in between, we're creating a very consistent arc from the ad copy to what they read on the landing page. If we're using, you know, store if we're if we're weaving in stories along the way, all of it is in service to the eventual outcome that we want, which is to produce a happy customer. So let's talk about why themes. And I wanted to spell some ideas about themes to we get we'll do that next. So why themes? First of all, when we say theme, we mean an organizing idea. Twyla Thark calls this the spine of a piece you may not even know, like may not even be it over, you may, you may know yourself when I say I misspoke their moment when I said you may not even know meaning the the person who's experienced it may may not be able to point to something and say, Oh, I get it. This was good versus evil. Like that's that's not what a theme really needs to be. The spine, the theme is is the thing that makes the beginning to the end makes sense to you as the creator into the prospect as being the person who experienced it. It's one cohesive story. And when I say story, don't start getting nervous about Oh, no, I don't I don't know how to tell stories. I don't mean story like that. I mean, it's just one cohesive piece of writing. There are eight parts to this. Six emails a landing page on a Facebook ad. We want all of that. And it will be, you know, conservatively ish it's somewhere between 8,000 and 10,000 words total. Those eight to 10,000 words all are along the same line. Right. That's that's why I've sort of drawn this with that that visual arc. It's we're not going to start talking about one thing in the ad and by the time we're in the emails, building a relationship, we're talking about something that's entirely different that doesn't have any relationship to what we talked about before. All of it is going to be along the same line. What a phrase I love from Robert Fritz is tension seeks resolution. So we're building tension along the way. And we can do that just like if we were writing a piece of fiction, we would we would know how the story ends. And to get to the ending, we would be creating tension along the way because the reader doesn't know how the story ends. That's very similar to what we're doing here is that the prospect who first enters your world is excited and engaged and they're being pulled forward because they don't know how it ends. They know that you have met them where they are by addressing speaking with empathy about a desire they have or fears they have or things that they want to accomplish. And you've pulled them into your world. They're they're brought forward because they're seeing things in a different way. And once that happens, they want to know what's next because that tension we're creating seeks resolution. They're pulled forward. It's also important to note that this is a systems view, not a linear view. I mentioned that earlier. And what's critical about that is that we are not going we're not trying to optimize the steps from one thing to the next. What everything that exists on this line is in service to the overall goal, which is generating happy customers. So we could get more clicks on a Facebook ad, for example, if we use curiosity in the headline, or if we we had some sort of intrigue that we didn't resolve until later. So we would we would be able to get more of something in the beginning that we think we want. But it would not likely produce more happy customers. So we would choose not to do it because everything that we're optimizing for along the entire arc of this journey is to produce the outcome that we care about most. So we're not going to put we're not going to use, you know, persuasion and other things to try to get people to buy who shouldn't, because that would create a customer but not a happy customer. So everything we're doing in this arc is in service to creating a happy customer. Let's talk about theme ideas. So literary themes are a place to start. And, you know, in the Sunday email that we sent last week, we mentioned some literary theme ideas and sort of a small mini course on theme. But that's just a starting point. You know, in the there are large themes in life, good versus evil, love, courage and perseverance. Like these are things you if you're we included those really thinking about this from the perspective of if you don't really know even where to start. The big literary themes are a great, great place to start courage and perseverance is a great one. For example, let's let's say for example that I have figured out a way to get stronger. I'm not the turn 50. So I'm an aging athlete and I figured out a way to get stronger with only 15 minutes a day, four days a week as a as an aging athlete as I transition into the next chapter in my life, whatever it is. So my, my, I go back to my prospects, I find people who are like me who want to do that and I need a theme. What could my theme be? Well, courage and perseverance is an easy theme. If I don't have anything else to work with courage and perseverance is an easy one to do. I could the whole arc from Facebook add to the promotional emails could be on that line of it takes courage to step forward and say, I want to get stronger. I'm willing to do the work. This is how I want to show up in the world. And it also takes perseverance to continue to do it day after day. And that that could be that's an easy theme to use. We could also do good versus evil here. Good versus evil works a lot in the health space because you could have a, you can make, you don't have to make another person or another entity, the evil entity. I mean, you can if there are big players in your marketplace who do things in ways that you think are not optimal, you could do that, but it's more making, making an idea. So Andre and I use good versus evil occasionally. And it's, I don't know that we do it deliberately, but it tends to emerge from this idea that there's a, there's a get rich quick vibe in the internet marketing world. And we've been in the game long enough to know that get rich slow is really how things generally work. And most get rich quick schemes. The person who gets rich is the person who's selling the scheme, not the customer. That's just our experience. So good versus evil works pretty well there. We don't cast stones at a particular person in the space, but we, we can present the evil in the space if we're using good versus evil. The evil is this idea that you can get rich overnight in your underwear on the beach drinking pinacoladas, right? That, that's just not true. Yes, a few people have done it, but by and large, that's not true. So the literary themes, great place to start if you don't have other options or if you just one of those resonates with you. Another theme that you can use is your story. This is particularly helpful if you've solved the same problems that your prospects want to solve, or you've achieved the same desire that a prospect wants to, wants to achieve. You can, the entire theme from beginning to end can be what you did to get to where you are, which is where they want to be. Michael Pollan talks about this, the author talks about this writing his books, and he doesn't want to be the expert on the stage, telling people how they should live or what they should do. What he said he likes to do is he likes to go on a journey and then tell his audience what that journey was like and what he learned along the way. So that's a, that's a sharing your story works really well. If you've done something. So we can just pick an example. Let's say for the sake of argument that I lost 50 pounds on a ketogenic diet. So, and let's to really further refine and dimensionalize this. Let's assume that I was 50 pounds overweight for a really long period of time. I tried everything, you know, low fat, low calorie, low carb, you name it. I tried it all vegan paleo, add it all up and I just I couldn't lose the weight. And then I discovered the ketogenic diet. And after three months, I'd lost 50 pounds. If that were true, and to be very clear, it needs to be true. If that were true, then that could be the thematic arc that connects this entire journey. I could hope I could tell start to tell my story in the Facebook ad. This is in the Facebook ad would be probably leading with where, where they are. This is a, I want to get into the details of this one because we'll cover it next week. But it's one of the choices we have to make is where do we start? Do we start by showing them the desired future reality or do we start by recognizing where they are? So let's just for the sake of argument say for this one that, that I start by recognizing where they are. And then I begin to tell my story. And I just hint at it. I open the loop that this, I describe for them what they're feeling. And I said, and I, I figured out, you know, I, I was you. I figured out what to do. Let me tell you about it. And then on the landing page before the opt in, because we don't want somebody to opt in just have curiosity to find out what the answer is. We want somebody to opt in or because they want to go to the next step after you've established somewhat of a relationship with them. So from the Facebook ad to the landing pages is a great area to make a mistake where you, you end the landing page with a cliffhanger. That they don't get to find out. Like in this example with the ketogenic diet, we could have a, you know, the, the cliffhanger would be I lost 50 pounds in three months after nothing else worked. Here's what I did. And we don't tell them what, what you did. And they need to opt in to find out. Well, that would give us a false signal because they're opting in a certain percentage are opting in just to find out what it was. Not because they're pulled forward. So if we were to open that loop in the Facebook ad, for example, we would want to close it in the landing page. We would want to be able to say in that part, those two parts, the Facebook ad to the landing page, I feel your pain because I felt it myself. There's empathy. Here's the journey that I went on. I tried this, this, this, this, and I'm sure they're nodding their head the same time thinking I've tried all those things too. And they didn't work. And then the transition is, but I finally, I finally found something that did work. And I would be, I would reveal it on the landing page. I try to ketogenic diet. And it was a, whatever, desperation, you know, just tell your story. I tried it. And for the first time, it actually worked. And I don't know that it works for everybody, but I know that it worked for me. And so that's a night. So if somebody gets to that point and they've already tried a ketogenic diet and they, and they said it didn't work for me, whatever, they're not going to opt in. They're not going to keep on the journey because they've, they already, they're already not going to be a happy customer. So it's good. It's good that we eliminate them early. So I didn't mean to get in the weeds there with how to use your story, but it just gives a little bit of an example of how you can use your story to pull somebody from beginning to end. And what you're selling when you get to the promotional emails at that point is you're selling the solution that you worked hard to create and explain, and that's what they get in exchange for the money. But you don't, you're not doing that at the landing page. You're not saying, you know, here's, here's, here's what I, my experience, this is what I did. I found out the magic solution, pay me $297 and I'll tell you what it is. Like that's too compressed. Yes, it does work. Sometimes it works in some markets better than others. But that's not what we're doing here. We are stacking probabilities in our favor by having this long arc of reinforcing tension producing pulling forward touch points where we're going to bring somebody over the course of six plus days. Because it's, if we assume we send one email per day for the six emails, and if they were to opt, they were to see the ad and opt in on day one, that would, they would get the first email. So this is at minimum six days over the entire course of this arc. So we're, we have significant opportunity to build this relationship and then show a solution. Another theme idea, if you've worked with people in the past and they've had success, tell their stories. If, if you're essentially, you're telling the story of somebody who's just like your prospect and how they got the result by working with you, you can make that story the spine of this entire arc. From beginning to end. My favorite, favorite, favorite arc to use is what I call the expert or the insider view. This doesn't work for everybody and it depends on how you define expert and a lot of it depends on your comfort level. But I'll tell you in broad strokes what it is and then you can decide if it's a good fit for you. So the expert or the insider theme is that somebody has spent a significant amount of time learning about how something works. Weight loss, making money online, you name it, how some, they, they, they know what matters and the prospects don't. So when somebody who doesn't know how something works, find somebody who spent a lot of time, it's somebody doesn't know how something works and they want to. And they bump into somebody who has spent a lot of time in that domain and they really understand how it works. That is, it creates such an interesting dynamic. The metaphor I like to use to explain this is the airplane seat. So imagine that you, you sit down on an airplane and somebody sits down next to you and by, through something that they say it is crystal clear to you that they are an ideal prospect for what you have to offer. You wouldn't immediately say, oh, it's interesting. I do, you know, I'm an expert in such and such and I do this thing and I here's, you know, give me your credit card and I'll sign you up for my course. Right. You'd never do that. The first thing that you would do is you would listen and you would hear them describing how this is what's going on. You'd ask some questions and you would know what questions to ask because you, you have a sense of their desires and their fears and what they're trying to accomplish. So you would ask some questions and you would establish some rapport in that conversation because you would be able to reflect back on them that you understand what they're going through. You feel their pain or you understand their desire. That's empathy. You would do that naturally just as a human being. You would do that. And then at some point you would transition because they, that person would recognize very quickly like, wow, you really know a lot about this. And then that would be the opportunity to say, well, actually for the last 15 years, I've been doing XYZ, whatever the topic is. I mean, I have a PhD or whatever it is. That's where you would naturally shift in credibility and to authority. Now you have their full attention, but you don't ask them to take out their credit card at that point. You would continue to build a relationship. You would ask them about, you would ask them some questions about how are they experiencing it and you would continue building the relationship. And at some point what that person wants to know most is what do I do next? How do, how do I continue to get benefit from you? That is the point at which he would say, well, you know, I've written a book, maybe. I've been on some podcasts, whatever it is. And I also have an online course that I created that helps people go from whatever and you would, at that point it would be perfectly natural. And at that point they would want to know that they most likely would want to buy it. The belief change here, the frame change here that's so profound is that they're lucky to have found you and they probably recognize that. That's how they would feel. So what we're trying to create in this thematic arc when we have this expert insider view is that sense of, wow, I've been trying to solve this for the longest time. And I am, I'm lucky to have found this person who is an expert in this issue that I'm trying to solve or this desire that I want to fulfill. That's such a beautiful framework if you can pull it off. Now, if you, if you create an offer or wrote a book and you've been doing something for six months and you don't really, if you're not the expert and you don't have the insider's view, don't try to make this one work. It's just too hard. We've certainly talked with people in our audience. Jonathan Baker comes to mind. Jonathan Baker is a cycling coach, an absolute top of his game. He is absolutely an expert and he can do this one exceptionally well because he's done the work. There are others in the group that are coming to mind too. Guys like Chris Lopez, an absolute leader in his field in strength work, kettlebell instruction. So somebody like that can use this frame to establish themselves as the expert because they are the expert. It's not bragging if you've done it. Right. And this isn't about bragging to be clear. This is just about being honest about what value you're able to create for someone who has a certain set of needs and how, how you understand what they're going through and how you could help them solve it. That's all the expert frame is one of my favorite ones. Last one we'll talk about as a theme idea. Anything that's counterintuitive counterintuitive thinking. I want to give credit to Rich Sheffrin for this one because this is something I've thought about for a long time, but I know I think better about it because of the way Rich Sheffrin has articulated it. But the idea with a counterintuitive thinking is that there are ways that people do things or there are established ways that we do things. And those things do not produce an optimal result. Yet it's what everybody does. And when you can show that the way that everybody is suggesting how to do something actually makes that something worse, you've then put all of your competitors and conventional wisdom into this bucket of things that are not the solution. So you become a category of one. So one of the examples I think is easiest to understand is around weight loss. And for those of you who know far more about this than I do, just recognize this as an example and what's not going to get wound up in the actual content of it. If I'm wrong, I'm wrong, but I think there's some truth to this. I think we can agree in broad strokes. There's truth to this. So if you believe that in order to lose weight, you absolutely have to kill yourself in the gym, high impact cardio, just absolutely wreck yourself in the gym. A case can be made that that is actually counterproductive. So here's the idea. What you think you think you're exercising like a maniac to burn a ton of calories so that you can lose weight. But what actually happens is you go to the gym and you kill yourself and then you're starving after and your stress hormones go through the roof. So the thing that you did to lose weight actually causes you to gain weight. That's a way that you can use counter like a counterintuitive thinking theme. Now you wouldn't say that outright in a Facebook ad or any of the individual components. These themes are built over the entirety of the arc and in the themes are based on belief somebody needs to have. There are lots of components and we're going to go through all of those. This is just the broad strokes overview in the beginning. What each of these themes is built over the course of eight touch points over minimum six days. So that they they're they're almost like a home base that we're coming to like think of that black line that connects Facebook ad to the last promotional email between those two audiences. Think of that as home base that we're always coming back to we're asking ourselves as we sit down to write a Facebook ad. What's the what's the theme that I'm working on. What's the organizing idea here like oh well it's it's really good versus evil right. It's it's the way people are doing things or the way people are being taught to do things in this particular market is to the benefit of the people who are teaching that but it doesn't actually work like that's the theme. So then when you get to the landing page into the relationship building emails and the promotional emails you're you're always returning to that same home base of am I am I still reinforcing that same message. Am I still on track here so from beginning to end. Am I focusing on the same set of arguments. Am I expressing them in different ways if I made my case so that by the time somebody is reading my promotional emails they're nodding their head up and down because they believe that I have a plan for the issues that I've already raised in this new world that I've shown them can exist. That's that's really what we're trying to accomplish here. So let's wrap up by talking about your homework. Every one of these workshop weeks is going to have homework. The way that you get the most benefit from this workshop is if you do the work it is a sprint. It is five weeks and there is a lot of work to do over those five weeks. And if you do the work you're going to wake up at the end of this process five weeks from now and you're going to have a customer producing asset. Maybe not the best one in the world. It's not may not be perfect but perfect is the enemy of complete perfect is the enemy of progress. So don't try to write every single bit of homework perfectly. Don't get caught up in that and don't get caught up in the intellectualization of this so that you're trying to diagram it in your mind and figure out just do the work. I can say that because I may I exactly the kind of person who would do that I would make every conceivable mistake that I'm suggesting that you not make. I would look for the intellectualization I would try to write the perfect copy. Don't do that. Do the homework each week as it's written and trust that at the end of the process you're going to have an asset that you're proud of and you're going to have an asset that's going to be either getting you happy customers or is going to be the start to getting you happy customers. So your homework for next week is first to dimensionalize the benefits your happy customers get working with you. What does that mean? It means sit down with a piece of paper and a pencil or a pen and just start writing. If somebody buys my product and does the work, whatever that is for you buys your pays for you as a service provider. If your service provider buys your product, if you sell a particular product, just if somebody does that, the benefits they get are colon and then just write them down and don't. Again, this don't worry about pros. Just write down everything that you can think of and think broadly. What does that what desires is that fulfilled? What do they get? What's the thing that they want that they get? Write that down and really get detailed. How do they feel when they get that thing? How do they change as a person when they've accomplished this? Don't hold back. Write it all out one after another. I think you'll want to go through that after and get it into a digital file as well, but you don't have to. You could highlight the ones that you think are the best. You just need those raw materials. That's the first set of raw materials that you need. The second part of your homework is to identify what someone needs to believe to accept the value of your offer. And there are two broad categories. They need to believe that you can help them, right? Clearly. So you can make a list of what's everything that they need to believe about you before they can accept the value of your offer. Let's just assume it's a weight loss offer. They have to believe that you've found a way that you lost weight, that you've shown other people how to lose weight with this system, that you've articulated the system in a way that other people can follow it. Just make that list. What do they need to believe? And then the second component that's critical and so often overlooked is that they have to believe that they can do it too. And if you leave that part out, you will leave a gaping hole in your ability to produce happy customers. Because at the end of the journey, somebody can nod up and down and think, you know what, I believe that that person lost 50 pounds. I believe that they've shown 500 other people how to lose 50 pounds or more. But I don't think I can do it. And if they don't believe they can do it, then they're not going to become a happy customer, plain and simple. So we have to address that as well. So the easy way to do that is to make a list of what they need to do and anything, any extra details. And get very specific. So if they need to commit to a seven day a week eating plan, make that clear. And say, if you can commit to a seven day a week eating plan and make the list, if you can do these things, then you can get success from this program. Now some people can't and that's perfectly okay because that if we get somebody who's not going to commit to buy, if they're not going to do the things that we know they need to do, we're going to get a customer, but we're not going to get a happy customer. And we want happy customers. So be clear about this. You don't have to use everyone. We're not going to create a checklist of disclaimers that they have to sign. But what we want to be able to find are the top things that we know that they need to accept about themselves in order to be successful so that we can reflect that back to them. And then finally choose three to five potential themes that you can build all of these pieces of content around. Don't don't get concerned about this one. Don't get particularly concerned about the word theme. It doesn't have to be a literary theme. Could be just choose something that you think connects the dots from a prospect to a happy customer. It could be again a story like your story, how you did it. And you just want to tell the world that story. That's a great theme. It could be in the absence of that. It could be again the expert one. If you can pull that one off, that's an outstanding one to use the expert, the insider. But if those are options for you for whatever reason, then start to go back to the literary themes. Think about things like good versus evil, courage and perseverance. Think of those broader terms. And if you still haven't found anything that really lights you up, think about stories that you like. Think about movies that you like, anything. We just need a toehold. We need something, a book that you enjoy, a podcast you listen to, a personality you think is interesting. We just need something that can become the organizing idea around which we will build this entire arc that is going to take people from not knowing you, prospects with desires and fears and things that they want to do to people who know you, who trust you, respect you, and even love you for the benefits that you've created in their lives. Next week, we're going to be focused on the Facebook ad. We will continue to reorient each of the component parts within the larger structure. But for next week, it's going to be the Facebook ad. You want to have your homework done. You do not need to submit your homework this week because there's nothing that we'll be looking at. If for some reason you do want to submit a question or something about the homework, post that in the comments in the Learning Academy. And if there's something that you don't want to share with everybody, for example, if you have a theme or a story that's specific to you that you want to just share with us, then just reply to the email announcing this module and we'll take a look at that. Each week, as homework is submitted, so starting, it'll be not next week, it'll be the, or I'm sorry, it'll be next week, by Sunday next week, when you submit your Facebook ad copy, we are going to select from people who submit their homework before Sunday at midnight Pacific time. We will, by Wednesday, have selected and then we'll just record our thoughts about a selection of those. We can't get to anybody. What we're going to focus on is giving feedback about homework that's broadly useful. Some of that will be, if we see something we think is done particularly well, we'll have a certain angle on it that we think is interesting, we'll talk about that. And it may also be things that were, something's off track and we want to help everybody course correct. We are not, this is not an opportunity for us to try to be smart and to show everyone how smart we are. This is an opportunity for us to be valuable and to create value for each of you. So please work, reach out. And just to clarify, because I know I was all over the place with the homework part, you don't need to submit any of this week's homework by Sunday, but if you would like to ask questions, ask them in the comments in the Academy, and if it's a question specific to something that you want to send to us, then just reply to the email associated with announcing this one. Thank you all very much. Good luck. Do the work.
